2026 · ACL 2026 · Agents

MobileWorld: Benchmarking Autonomous Mobile Agents in Agent-User Interactive and MCP-Augmented Environments

AndroidWorld's leaderboard broke 90% and stopped discriminating between agents. MobileWorld resets the difficulty with 201 long-horizon, cross-app tasks that also make agents ask clarifying questions and reach for external tools via MCP — and even the best system tops out at 51.7%.

Quyu Kong, Xu Zhang, Zhenyu Yang, Nolan Gao, Chen Liu, Panrong Tong, Chenglin Cai, Hanzhang Zhou, Jianan Zhang, Liangyu Chen, Zhidan Liu, Steven Hoi, Yue Wang · Tongyi Lab, Alibaba Group / HKUST (GZ) / University of Florida
venue ACL 2026 citations 39 topic Agents source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-07
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

AndroidWorld became the default mobile-GUI-agent benchmark because it is reproducible and deterministically scored: 116 tasks across 20 real apps, dynamically parameterized so the same task can be re-sampled with different arguments. That made it useful — and, three years on, that usefulness ran out. State-of-the-art agentic frameworks now clear 90%+ success on its leaderboard, which the authors argue can no longer separate genuine capability gains from noise.

Saturation is only the most visible problem. The authors identify three structural gaps in the whole family of prior mobile-agent benchmarks (AndroidWorld, A3, SPA-Bench, AndroidLab, and others), summarized in the paper's Table 1: (1) tasks are short-horizon and largely single-app, and the app roster skips categories central to real usage — e-commerce, enterprise communication — that require either mandatory authentication or produce opaque internal state prior benchmarks can't verify without noisy "MLLM-as-a-judge" grading; (2) every prior benchmark assumes the user instruction is fully specified, when real mobile requests are routinely vague ("send this to Kevin" with no email on file) and a competent agent needs to ask rather than guess; (3) none evaluate the Model Context Protocol (MCP), even though MCP has rapidly become the standard way agents reach external tools, and the community has started debating how GUI actions and MCP calls should be combined in a single agent loop — a combination no benchmark tests.

MobileWorld is built to close all three gaps in one environment: harder and longer tasks, an agent-user clarification loop, and MCP-augmented tasks — all still scored deterministically via self-hosted app backends rather than a judge model.

// how it works

The method

MobileWorld defines a mobile-GUI task as a POMDP over screenshots + a natural-language instruction, standard mobile UI actions (click, drag, scroll, navigate, …), and a binary completion reward. On top of the AndroidWorld-style GUI-only tasks (task completion and information retrieval), it adds two new task families that need new actions in the agent's action space:

  • Agent-user interaction tasks (45, 22.4% of the set). Annotators write a fully-specified task, then deliberately strip out a required detail (e.g. replace "kevin_zhang@example.com" with just "Kevin") and verify the missing fact isn't recoverable from the device state. A GPT-4.1-based simulated user, seeded with exactly the withheld fact and instructed to refuse off-task questions, answers the agent's ask_user queries — turning missing-information handling into something measurable rather than something the agent can silently hallucinate past.
  • MCP-augmented tasks (40, 19.9% of the set). Annotators curate tasks solvable only through one of 64 MCP tools across 5 servers (GitHub, Amap Maps, Jina AI, StockStar, arXiv), then chain a GUI step onto the MCP output (e.g. pull a GitHub README via the GitHub MCP tool, then compose and send a summary email through the native Mail app) — forcing the agent to choose between GUI navigation and a direct tool call and combine both in one trajectory.

To keep evaluation deterministic even for apps that would otherwise need "MLLM-as-a-judge" grading, MobileWorld self-hosts production-grade open-source stand-ins for commercial apps it can't authenticate into or introspect — Mattermost for Slack, Mastodon for X/Twitter, Mall4Uni for e-commerce — modifying their source and gaining direct backend-database access. Four verification modes then check task completion: textual answer matching, backend-database queries, local on-device storage inspection (via rooted-emulator ADB), and custom application callbacks. The whole stack runs in Docker-in-Docker containers with AVD snapshots so every run starts from an identical device state.

To baseline the new task types, the authors build a planner-executor agentic framework: a planner (any general-purpose VLM) reads the screenshot and history and picks the next action in natural language ("the 'Send' button at the bottom right"), while a separate grounding model — UI-Ins-7B, from the same lab's own prior work — converts that description into pixel coordinates. The action space is extended with ask_user (routes a query to the simulated-user agent) and mcp_call (invokes a named MCP tool with structured parameters), so both new task families run inside one closed decision loop rather than a bolted-on side-channel.

Five metrics quantify behavior beyond a single pass/fail number: overall Success Rate and per-category SR (GUI-Only, Interaction, MCP), Average Completion Steps, Average User Queries, User Interaction Quality (UIQ — success divided by queries invoked, penalizing both failed and unnecessarily-triggered clarifications), and Average MCP Tool Calls.

UIQ = Σi∈Interact (si / ci if ci>0, else 0)|Interact| + |Triggered| // c_i = ask_user calls on task i, s_i = success indicator; the denominator also counts non-interaction tasks where the agent triggered an unnecessary ask_user, so chatty models get penalized twice
// what they found

The results

The headline comparison, in Figure 1: against AndroidWorld's 90%+ SOTA success rate, MobileWorld's best system reaches only 51.7%. Tasks average 27.8 completion steps versus AndroidWorld's 14.3 (+13.5 steps, and right-skewed — a large share of MobileWorld tasks run past 20 steps), and 62.2% of tasks are multi-app versus AndroidWorld's 9.5%.

Table 6 breaks the leaderboard down by architecture and task category (max 50 steps per task). GPT-5 + UI-Ins-7B tops the agentic-framework category at 51.7% overall (54.0% GUI-Only, 62.2% User-Interaction, 51.6% MCP), ahead of Gemini-3-Pro + UI-Ins-7B (46.3%) and Claude-4.5-Sonnet + UI-Ins-7B (43.8%). Among end-to-end GUI models, Doubao-1.5-UI-TARS leads at 20.9% overall (26.3% GUI-Only, 32.4% User-Interaction, not evaluated on MCP), with the rest — GUI-Owl-7B/32B, UI-Venus-7B/72B, Qwen3-VL-8B/32B/235B-A22B, GELab-Zero-4B — clustered between 4.5% and 10.9%. On MCP-augmented tasks specifically, only the three agentic frameworks and the Qwen3-VL family attempt tool calls at all; Qwen3-VL's MCP success rate is 0.0–5.4% despite averaging 2.3–3.8 tool calls per task, because a large share of its generated tool names and arguments are simply wrong.

ModelOverallGUI-Only (116)User-Int (45)MCP (40)
GPT-5 + UI-Ins-7B51.754.062.251.6
Gemini-3-Pro + UI-Ins-7B46.355.624.448.6
Claude-4.5-Sonnet + UI-Ins-7B43.847.837.850.0
Doubao-1.5-UI-TARS20.926.332.4—
GELab-Zero-4B10.916.16.7—
UI-Venus-72B10.416.4——
Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B9.512.84.45.4
GUI-Owl-7B4.57.7——

Table 7's efficiency and interaction-quality metrics add texture the raw SR misses. Gemini-3-Pro + UI-Ins-7B is the most efficient (24.2 avg steps), but GPT-5 + UI-Ins-7B is the most judicious clarifier: 1.11 average queries per interaction task, a UIQ of 0.40 (best of the three), and correspondingly the highest User-Interaction SR (62.2%). Every model's UIQ sits well below its raw User-Interaction SR — Doubao-1.5-UI-TARS is the starkest case, at 32.4% SR but only 0.13 UIQ, meaning most of its clarification queries are redundant or off-target rather than genuinely necessary. On MCP tasks, tool-call volume tracks success: Gemini-3-Pro averages 2.63 calls/task (48.6% MCP SR), GPT-5 2.23 calls (51.6% SR), Claude-4.5-Sonnet 1.91 calls (50.0% SR).

A manual failure-mode sweep surfaces five recurring gaps: agents hallucinating missing facts instead of asking (e.g. inventing "Shanghai" as a departure city rather than asking the user); MCP tool responses (a 20K-token PDF dump, in one case) overflowing the context window and burying the needed fact; no working long-term memory, so an agent renaming files by a shared prefix loses track of which it already renamed and re-touches them; weak multi-step arithmetic (summing the top-3 cart prices, one item at a time); and no grounding in real-world time/location unless it's spelled out in the prompt.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

MobileWorld is a genuine step up in task realism and a real, deterministic answer to "MLLM-as-a-judge is noisy" — the self-hosted-backend design is the right instinct. But three aspects of the setup deserve real scrutiny before treating 51.7% as a clean measure of general mobile-agent capability.

The SOTA baseline shares its grounding model — and its authors — with the benchmark. Every agentic-framework row in Table 6, including the winning GPT-5 + UI-Ins-7B at 51.7%, is paired with UI-Ins-7B as the coordinate-grounding executor. UI-Ins is not a neutral third-party model: its own reference (Chen et al., 2025, arXiv:2510.20286) lists Liangyu Chen, Hanzhang Zhou, Chenglin Cai, Jianan Zhang, Panrong Tong, Quyu Kong, Xu Zhang, and Chen Liu as authors — essentially the same author roster as MobileWorld itself, released two months earlier from the same lab. That doesn't invalidate the number, but it does mean the headline SOTA result pairs a benchmark with a grounding model built in-house by the people who built the benchmark, while every end-to-end competitor (GUI-Owl, UI-Venus, Doubao, Qwen3-VL, GELab-Zero) is a genuinely external system evaluated as-is. The paper doesn't report how an outside grounding model (or no modular split at all) would change the agentic-framework numbers.

The "Overall" SR appears to silently penalize models for lacking capabilities the paper excused them from having. Section 4.3 explicitly says MCP-augmented tasks are dropped for models with no tool-invocation support, and interaction tasks are dropped for models with no ask_user action — yet Equation 1 defines Overall SR as a single sum over all N = 201 tasks. Working the numbers backward confirms it: GUI-Owl-7B's GUI-Only SR of 7.7% over 116 tasks implies ≈8.9 successes, and 8.9/201 ≈ 4.4% — matching its reported 4.5% Overall almost exactly, which only works if the 85 untested interaction/MCP tasks are silently scored as failures rather than excluded from the denominator. That means part of the 51.7% vs. 20.9% gap between agentic frameworks and end-to-end models is mechanical: end-to-end models are structurally guaranteed to lose ~42% of the score mass for tasks they were never given the action space to attempt, which is a different claim than "agentic frameworks are more than twice as capable."

The two hardest task categories carry the least statistical weight. User-Interaction and MCP-augmented results — the paper's own headline contribution — are each computed over only 40–45 tasks. A percentage like GPT-5's 62.2% User-Interaction SR (28 of 45 tasks) or a competitor's reported 0.0% MCP SR moves by multiple points per task; no confidence intervals or repeated-run variance are reported (models are run once, at temperature 0.0), so category-level rankings among close competitors should be read as directional rather than precise.

baselines

The winning agentic framework's grounding model (UI-Ins-7B) comes from the same lab and largely the same author list as MobileWorld itself, while every end-to-end competitor is a fully external system — an asymmetry the paper doesn't flag or control for.

contamination

No explicit train/test leakage check is reported for the fine-tuned end-to-end baselines (UI-TARS, UI-Venus, GUI-Owl, GELab-Zero) against MobileWorld's own construction methodology or app roster.

what's missed

Table 6's single "Overall" SR appears to fold untested task categories in as failures rather than excluding them, inflating the agentic-vs-end-to-end capability gap; and the two novel categories (Interaction, MCP) rest on only 40–45 tasks each with no reported run-to-run variance.

would raise confidence

Re-run agentic frameworks with an external grounding model; report Overall SR both with and without excluded categories; add multiple seeds/temperature>0 runs with confidence intervals on the Interaction and MCP subsets.

// bottom line

MobileWorld correctly diagnoses that AndroidWorld saturated and that agent-user interaction plus MCP tool use are the right next frontier, and its self-hosted deterministic verification is a real methodological upgrade over judge-model scoring. But the flagship 51.7% pairs the benchmark's own grounding model with the winning framework, the headline capability gap is partly an artifact of how untested categories are scored, and the two new task families — the paper's real contribution — are each measured on a few dozen tasks with no variance reporting. Treat the ranking of agentic frameworks vs. end-to-end models as directionally right, but don't over-read the exact percentages.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. MobileWorld replaces AndroidWorld's saturated 90%+ leaderboard with 201 longer, more cross-app tasks (27.8 avg steps vs. 14.3, 62.2% multi-app vs. 9.5%) — and the best system, GPT-5 + UI-Ins-7B, still only reaches 51.7%.
  2. It adds two genuinely new task families evaluated with deterministic, self-hosted verification instead of judge models: agent-user clarification dialogue (22.4% of tasks) and MCP tool invocation (19.9%) — where end-to-end GUI models mostly score under 10% or fail to invoke tools at all.
  3. Read the numbers with two caveats: the SOTA framework's grounding model (UI-Ins-7B) shares authors with the benchmark, and the Overall SR formula appears to count untested categories as failures — both push the reported agentic-vs-end-to-end gap wider than a controlled comparison would show.
